text: The surgeon opens the gastrosplenic omentum, extending the dissection down to the left crus. The pars flaccida is opened, exposing the right crus, and the anterior portion of the hiatus is being exposed.

The surgeon carefully avoids injuring the anterior vagus nerve while creating a retroesophageal window. Pin rows are placed for retraction, and mediastinal dissection is performed. The posterior esophageal mediastinal mobilization is conducted, with attention to the epiphrenic diverticulum located anteriorly.

The surgeon navigates towards the epiphrenic diverticulum, which is adherent to the left pleura, making visualization challenging due to its anterior location. Using a third-degree camera, the surgeon carefully manipulates the thick-walled diverticulum, ensuring minimal pressure on the heart to avoid affecting blood pressure.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of a six-centimeter epiphrenic diverticulum, focusing on the superior portion and dissecting down to the neck. The thickened mucosa is visible between the muscle wall. The anterior vagus nerve is identified and teased away from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon attempts to preserve the anterior vagus nerve while dissecting the superior portion of the epiphrenic diverticulum, aiming to reach the neck of the esophageal diverticulum. The muscles of the esophagus are visibly split, and the anterior vagus nerve is pushed aside during the dissection. The surgeon decides to perform an anterior vagotomy to resect the diverticulum, continuing to free it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses cigar sponges to assist in dissecting the thick tissues surrounding the epiphrenic diverticulum, aiming to reach the neck of the diverticulum. The anterior vagus nerve is visible in the surgical field, and preparations are made for its resection.

The surgeon releases the superior portion of the epiphrenic diverticulum and prepares to resect it, sending the specimen to pathology. An endo loop is used to close the area, but it causes an obstruction, preventing the passage of a scope to the stomach. Blunt scissors are used to cut the tissue, making the process challenging.

The surgeon uses a DEMA stitch suture to stabilize the liver, which keeps flipping down during the procedure. The epiphrenic diverticulum is observed to be filled with cabbage particles, requiring extensive cleaning of its mouth before placing a staple line adjacent to it.

The surgeon uses suction to clean the contents from the epiphrenic diverticulum, allowing for the passage of a scope into the stomach. A lighted bougie is used to navigate, avoiding insertion into the diverticulum. The surgeon opens the diverticulum to remove its contents, facilitating better clamping and ensuring no particles obstruct the staple line.

The surgeon uses a 60mm endo GIE stapler to reinforce the staple line on the epiphrenic diverticulum. As the stapler clamps down, particles are visibly expelled from the diverticulum. The stapling process is performed with sequential firing, ensuring a controlled closure.

The surgeon removes the fat pad and performs a myotomy along the staple line, approximately three centimeters below the gastroesophageal junction. The mucosa is visibly exposed, and a lighted bougie is used to assist with the procedure.

The surgeon uses a lighted bougie to assist with the procedure, ensuring not to spread too wide with the ligature to avoid mucosal injury. After removing the bougie, a leak test is performed, which is negative. The surgeon then performs a posterior chiroplasty using either an O or number one ethebon suture in a figure-eight fashion, followed by a simple suture. A door fundoplication is completed using slip knots in the usual fashion.
